name: ÆRTH
description: Planetary AI digital twin using blockchain and remote sensing.
about: ÆRTH is building a living digital twin of Earth that combines satellite remote sensing, blockchain verification, and AI to model planetary systems in near-real time. As one of the most ambitious SPACESHIP projects in the ecosystem, it embodies the conviction that governing the Earth well requires seeing it whole — not as a collection of national datasets, but as a single interconnected system. If it delivers on its promise, ÆRTH could become foundational infrastructure for any serious planetary governance architecture.
imageAlt: Underwater reef ecosystem monitored by ÆRTH platform

name: Agenda Gotsch
description: Syntropic farming education and practice platform founded by Ernst Gotsch, promoting regenerative agroforestry systems through training, demonstration farms, and a global practitioner network.
about: Ernst Götsch's syntropic agroforestry system is more than a farming method — it is a philosophy of working with ecological succession rather than against it. Agenda Gotsch spreads this approach through training, demonstration farms, and a global practitioner network, showing that abundance can be regenerated even in degraded landscapes. In the planetary governance puzzle, they represent a living proof-of-concept: that GARDEN-scale transformation is not only possible but already happening, one farm at a time.
imageAlt: Ernst Götsch in his syntropic agroforestry farm

name: Agroecology Coalition
description: Global multi-stakeholder coalition of governments, research institutions, civil society and private sector actors advocating food system transformation through agroecology's 13 core principles.
about: The Agroecology Coalition represents the institutional infrastructure for food system transformation — bringing together governments, researchers, civil society, and private actors around a shared scientific and political framework. By advancing agroecology's 13 principles in policy spaces from the UN Food Systems Summit to national farm programmes, it bridges the gap between grassroots practice and global governance. In the planetary puzzle, it is the GARDEN-ASSEMBLY interface: the place where ecological farming becomes food policy.
imageAlt: Community members planting crops using agroecological methods
fundingDisclosure: EU, Gates Foundation, CGIAR member funders

name: Agroecology Europe
description: European association advancing agroecology through transdisciplinary research, farmer-to-farmer knowledge exchange, and policy engagement across the EU.
imageAlt: Barcelona Letter on agroecology in Europe
about: Agroecology Europe bridges the gap between grassroots farming practice and European policy — connecting researchers, farmers, civil society, and policymakers around agroecology as both a scientific framework and a political project. By building transdisciplinary networks and advocating within EU agricultural policy processes, they work to shift the enormous subsidy and regulatory architecture of European farming toward ecological principles. In the planetary governance puzzle, they represent the GARDEN-ASSEMBLY interface applied to food systems at continental scale.

name: Alliance for Water Stewardship
description: Global multi-stakeholder network operating the International Water Stewardship Standard, engaging industry, civil society and governments in collective water governance.
imageAlt: Alliance for Water Stewardship standard launch promotional imagery
about: Water governance is one of the most pressing planetary challenges of the coming century — and the Alliance for Water Stewardship has developed the international infrastructure to address it. Their Water Stewardship Standard provides a framework through which industry, civil society, and government can collectively govern shared water resources at the catchment level. In the planetary governance context, they represent the ASSEMBLY orientation applied to one of Earth's most critical commons: demonstrating that multi-stakeholder governance of shared water systems is practically achievable.

name: Arbonics
description: Estonian-Nordic forest carbon credit platform using satellite imagery and AI-driven growth models for transparent monitoring, reporting and verification (MRV) of forest carbon projects. Launched in Sweden 2025 via Treebula partnership.
imageAlt: Dense forest landscape monitored by Arbonics platform
about: Arbonics addresses a foundational problem in forest carbon markets: the lack of reliable, verifiable, and transparent monitoring. By using satellite imagery and AI-driven growth models for MRV (monitoring, reporting, and verification), they provide the data infrastructure that allows forest carbon projects to be trusted by buyers, regulators, and communities. In the planetary governance puzzle, they represent the SPACESHIP-GARDEN interface: the technology layer that makes forest stewardship legible to financial and regulatory systems.

name: BalticWaters
description: Specialized environmental foundation focused on ecological restoration and preservation of the Baltic Sea, bridging scientific theory and practical implementation through research, policy advocacy, and knowledge dissemination.
imageAlt: Cod research publication by BalticWaters foundation
about: The Baltic Sea is one of the world's most stressed marine ecosystems — overdosed with nutrients, depleted of oxygen, contaminated with legacy pollutants — and BalticWaters is dedicated to its recovery. By bridging scientific research with policy advocacy and restoration practice across the countries sharing this enclosed sea, they embody the GARDEN-ASSEMBLY intersection: the conviction that ecological restoration requires both ecological knowledge and governance coordination among multiple nations with competing interests.

name: Beckley Foundation
description: UK-based research foundation pioneering scientific research into psychedelics and consciousness, while advocating evidence-based drug policy reform globally.
imageAlt: Cosmic galaxy visualization representing consciousness research
about: The Beckley Foundation works at an unusual intersection of neuroscience, consciousness research, and policy reform — pioneering the scientific study of psychedelic-assisted therapy and advocating for evidence-based drug policy globally. In the planetary governance context, they represent the MYSTERIES orientation at its most scientifically grounded: the investigation of consciousness and inner transformation as dimensions of human potential that current legal and political frameworks unnecessarily constrain. Their work on altered states speaks to the inner ecology that may be as important as the outer.

name: Blockchain for Climate Foundation
description: Canadian foundation bridging blockchain technology with UNFCCC climate policy, convening governments, NGOs and Indigenous leaders to explore transparent and interoperable carbon accounting infrastructure.
imageAlt: Blockchain for Climate Foundation visual identity
about: The Blockchain for Climate Foundation works at the intersection of digital infrastructure and international climate policy — exploring how transparent, interoperable blockchain systems could transform the way carbon credits are tracked and traded under the UNFCCC framework. By convening governments, NGOs, and indigenous leaders around the technical design of carbon accounting systems, they address a critical governance bottleneck: the absence of reliable infrastructure for Article 6 carbon market cooperation. They represent the SPACESHIP-ASSEMBLY interface in climate finance.

name: Buckminster Fuller Institute
description: Advancing Buckminster Fuller's vision of design science as a tool for solving civilisation-scale challenges — making the world work for everyone within planetary limits.
about: The Buckminster Fuller Institute champions the idea that our most pressing global problems — resource scarcity, ecological collapse, inequality — can be solved through intelligent design rather than political struggle alone. Drawing on Fuller's concept of 'doing more with less,' BFI runs design challenges and awards that recognise work at the intersection of systems thinking, technology, and ecology. In the planetary governance puzzle, BFI represents the conviction that the architecture of our built world is itself a form of governance — and that redesigning it is one of the most powerful levers available.
imageAlt: Inside the Montreal geodesic dome

name: C40 Cities
description: A global network of mayors from nearly 100 major cities committed to urgent climate action, demonstrating that cities are among the most effective actors in the planetary transition.
about: While national governments often move slowly, cities concentrate both the urgency of climate impacts and the practical capacity for rapid action. C40 Cities connects mayors to share policy, data, and political will — turning climate commitments into measurable change in transport, buildings, energy, and food systems. For planetary governance, C40 represents the critical insight that sub-national actors — not just nation-states — are key nodes in any effective global governance architecture.

name: Capital Institute
description: Think tank developing the theory and practice of regenerative economics, publishing the '8 Principles of a Regenerative Economy' framework and the Finance for a Regenerative World paper series.
about: Capital Institute works on one of the hardest problems in the transition: how to redirect finance — the circulatory system of the modern economy — toward regenerative rather than extractive ends. Their 'Regenerative Finance' framework draws on living systems principles to redesign how capital flows, arguing that finance must be embedded in place and ecology rather than abstracted from it. In the planetary governance context, they represent the SPACESHIP-GARDEN interface: using systems thinking to reimagine the financial architecture that shapes what gets built and what gets destroyed.
imageAlt: Regenerative Economics book launch
fundingDisclosure: Foundation grants, individual donors; limited public disclosure

name: Centre for International Governance Innovation
description: Canadian think tank conducting research on international governance, digital economy, and global security, with focus on reforming multilateral institutions for contemporary challenges.
imageAlt: CIGI campus and research centre
about: CIGI is one of the world's leading think tanks on the reform and renewal of global governance institutions. From AI governance to multilateral reform, digital economy regulation to global security architecture, their research addresses the structural inadequacy of current international institutions for contemporary planetary challenges. In the governance ecosystem, they represent the SPACESHIP-ASSEMBLY interface at its most institutionally focused: rigorous analysis of what needs to change in the architecture of global governance, and how.

name: Citizen OS
description: Estonian civic tech non-profit developing a free, open-source digital participation platform for participatory democracy. Founded in 2015 from environmental activism movements, enables communities to discuss, deliberate, and vote on decisions.
imageAlt: Citizen OS community participation event
about: Citizen OS has built one of the world's most user-friendly open-source platforms for digital democracy — enabling communities, organisations, and governments to host structured deliberation, gather public input, and run participatory decision-making online. Born from Estonian civic activism, the platform has been adopted internationally and is freely available to any organisation that wants to use it. In the planetary governance context, they represent the SPACESHIP-ASSEMBLY interface at its most accessible: reducing the technical barrier to genuine digital participation.

name: Civic Tech Sweden
description: Decentralized network of individuals, organizations, and public sector entities leveraging digital technology for public benefit. Closely intertwined with Open Knowledge Sweden.
imageAlt: Civic Tech Sweden community meetup and collaboration
about: Civic Tech Sweden builds the connective tissue between Sweden's democratic innovation community — linking the individuals, organisations, and public sector entities experimenting with technology as a tool for civic empowerment. By nurturing this network and sharing knowledge across sectors, they accelerate the adoption of participatory technologies in Swedish governance. In the planetary governance ecosystem, they represent the SPACESHIP-ASSEMBLY orientation applied locally: the conviction that digital tools should serve democratic values, and that a national community of practice is how that conviction gets implemented.

name: Climate Action Data Trust
description: Multi-stakeholder initiative providing open, blockchain-based metadata infrastructure to prevent double counting of carbon credits across registries, linking Verra, Gold Standard and national registries through interoperable data layers.
imageAlt: Climate Action Data Trust homepage banner
about: The Climate Action Data Trust solves one of carbon market governance's most stubborn problems: double counting, where the same emissions reduction is claimed by multiple parties. By building open, blockchain-based metadata infrastructure that links registries like Verra and Gold Standard, the Trust creates a shared ledger that makes carbon claims auditable and trustworthy. In the planetary governance context, they represent the SPACESHIP-GARDEN interface at its most technical but most consequential: the data infrastructure without which carbon markets cannot function as credible climate governance tools.

name: Climate Interactive
description: MIT-affiliated nonprofit running the World Climate Simulation — a role-playing negotiation game putting participants in a fictitious UN climate summit, backed by the En-ROADS systems dynamics model providing real-time feedback on policy choices. Used in 100+ countries.
about: Climate Interactive's En-ROADS simulator makes the complexity of climate systems navigable for policymakers, educators, and citizens — letting users explore in real time how different combinations of policies affect global temperature. By making the planetary system interactive and comprehensible, they transform climate governance from an expert domain into a shared intellectual commons. In the planetary puzzle, they represent the SPACESHIP orientation at its most democratic: using simulation to give everyone the tools to think systemically about our collective future.

name: Climate Justice Alliance
description: US-based coalition of 90+ frontline community organisations and support groups building a just transition away from extractive systems through community-led governance and regenerative economies.
imageAlt: Climate Justice Alliance logo and branding
about: The Climate Justice Alliance centres the voices of frontline communities — indigenous peoples, communities of colour, workers — in the climate transition, insisting that justice and ecology cannot be separated. Their Just Transition framework has become one of the most widely adopted models in the global climate movement, arguing that the shift to a regenerative economy must be led by those most harmed by the extractive economy. In the planetary governance puzzle, they represent the GARDEN-ASSEMBLY interface at its most politically radical: the claim that any planetary governance worth the name must be grounded in the experiences and leadership of those who have borne the costs of ecological destruction.

name: Climate TRACE
description: An independent coalition using AI and satellite imagery to track greenhouse gas emissions from every major facility on Earth — bringing radical transparency to global climate accountability.
about: For decades, emissions data relied on national self-reporting — creating a systemic blind spot in climate governance. Climate TRACE changes that by building a real-time, independent ledger of who is emitting what and where, down to individual facilities. This infrastructure of accountability is foundational to any credible system of planetary governance: you cannot govern what you cannot see, and Climate TRACE is building the nervous system that makes planetary emissions visible.
imageAlt: Industrial emissions visualization from satellite data

name: Co-Intelligence Institute
description: Founded by Tom Atlee in 1996 to research and support the emergence of collective wisdom in groups and societies. Developed the theory of co-intelligence, the citizen deliberative council model, and the Wise Democracy Pattern Language. Works at the intersection of group process, dialogue, and democratic theory to ask how humanity can become collectively wise enough to handle its challenges.
about: The Co-Intelligence Institute has spent three decades researching one of the most important questions for planetary governance: how do human groups — and eventually, human civilisation as a whole — become collectively wise? Tom Atlee's work on citizen deliberative councils, co-intelligence, and the Wise Democracy Pattern Language provides both a theoretical framework and practical toolkit for designing governance systems that can access the distributed wisdom of whole communities. They represent the ASSEMBLY orientation at its most philosophical: a sustained inquiry into the conditions under which collective intelligence actually emerges.

name: Coalition Clean Baltic
description: Politically independent non-profit network uniting 28 environmental organizations across 11 Baltic Sea countries. Won Nordic Council Nature and Environment Prize for model approach to uniting environmental bodies across geopolitical divides.
about: Coalition Clean Baltic has spent decades building the civil society infrastructure for Baltic Sea governance — uniting environmental organisations from 11 countries into a coherent advocacy force that can hold governments and industries accountable. Winner of the Nordic Council's Nature and Environment Prize, they embody the GARDEN-ASSEMBLY intersection at the regional scale: demonstrating that environmental advocacy can bridge geopolitical divides and create genuine cross-border civil society power. They are a model for how regional commons can be governed when national interests diverge.

name: Curve Labs
description: Web3 development lab building institutional ReFi technology. Core technical partner for Kolektivo Framework and tokenised ecological assets.
imageAlt: Artistic visualization of digital governance systems
about: Curve Labs provides the technical infrastructure for the regenerative finance (ReFi) ecosystem — building the smart contracts, tokenomics designs, and Web3 governance tools that allow communities and projects to coordinate around ecological and social goals using blockchain. As core technical partner for projects like Kolektivo, they represent the SPACESHIP layer that makes the ReFi vision computationally real. In the planetary governance puzzle, they occupy the crucial engineering role: turning regenerative economic theory into functioning decentralised systems.

name: Dark Matter Labs
description: A research and design studio reimagining the foundational institutions of our civilisation — law, land, finance, and insurance — as tools for ecological and social regeneration.
about: Dark Matter Labs works at the intersection of institutional innovation and ecological transition, asking: what would law, land ownership, and finance look like if they were designed for a living planet? Their work on 'smart covenants' and system demonstrators (in cities like Lund and Stockholm with Viable Cities) offers concrete experiments in redesigning the rules that shape how land, capital, and risk are distributed. In the planetary governance puzzle, they represent the crucial recognition that transforming institutions is as important as changing behaviour.
imageAlt: Dark Matter Labs systems mapping
fundingDisclosure: EU Horizon, Bloomberg Philanthropies, project fees

name: Democracy Without Borders
description: A global advocacy organisation campaigning for a United Nations Parliamentary Assembly — a directly elected global chamber that would give citizens a formal voice in international governance.
about: Current global governance is conducted almost entirely by states and their officials, with citizens having no direct representation at the international level. Democracy Without Borders argues that this democratic deficit is a fundamental flaw in our planetary architecture, and campaigns for a UNPA as the first step toward genuinely democratic world governance. They represent the ASSEMBLY dimension of planetary governance at its most ambitious scale: not just better deliberation within nations, but democratic legitimacy for the global institutions that shape everyone's future.
imageAlt: World parliament concept

name: Demokrati Garage
description: Independent NGO and physical gathering place in Copenhagen functioning as a DIY workshop and development platform for democratic innovation. Co-founded by leadership of We Do Democracy.
imageAlt: Community event at Demokrati Garage
about: Demokrati Garage is a physical home for democratic innovation in Copenhagen — a space where theorists, practitioners, activists, and curious citizens can meet, experiment, and build new approaches to collective decision-making. Connected to the broader Nordic deliberation ecosystem through its links with We Do Democracy, it operates as a DIY laboratory for democracy: the kind of space where the next generation of democratic practices gets prototyped before it scales. They represent the ASSEMBLY orientation at its most experimental and welcoming.

name: Demos Helsinki
description: Independent think tank and policy institute focusing on societal transformation, foresight, and long-term governance. Bridges foresight frameworks and practical policymaking.
imageAlt: Demos Helsinki team and organization visual
fundingDisclosure: EU projects, Nordic Council, Finnish government
about: Demos Helsinki is Finland's leading think tank on societal transformation — combining foresight research, policy design, and systems thinking to help governments and organisations navigate the deep uncertainty of the 21st century. Their work on long-term governance, anticipatory policymaking, and the future of democracy contributes directly to the intellectual infrastructure of the planetary governance movement. In the puzzle, they represent the SPACESHIP-ASSEMBLY interface: rigorous analytical capacity in service of governance innovation for a world in rapid, complex transition.

name: Destination Earth (DestinE)
description: An EU flagship initiative building a high-precision digital twin of Earth to model climate, weather, and planetary systems — supporting better decision-making for the ecological transition.
about: Destination Earth is building what may become one of the most consequential governance tools of the century: a living, ultra-precise simulation of Earth's climate and natural systems. By enabling policymakers to model the consequences of different choices before implementing them, it transforms planetary governance from reactive crisis management to anticipatory stewardship. This is SPACESHIP thinking at the civilisational scale — using our most advanced modelling capabilities to see the Earth as a whole system that can be understood and, carefully, guided.
imageAlt: Destination Earth digital twin

name: Digidem Lab
description: Swedish worker-owned cooperative specializing in design and development of methodologies for political and democratic innovation. Executed the historic Swedish Citizens' Assembly on Climate in 2024.
imageAlt: Digidem Lab workshop on digital democratic participation
about: Digidem Lab is Sweden's specialist in participatory democracy methodology — a worker-owned cooperative that designs and executes deliberative processes, including the landmark 2024 Swedish Citizens' Assembly on Climate. By combining technical expertise in digital participation tools with deep knowledge of deliberative methods, they provide the practical capacity to implement meaningful public participation at scale. In the planetary governance context, they represent the ASSEMBLY orientation made operational in the Nordic context: turning democratic theory into practice, one assembly at a time.

name: Doughnut Economics Action Lab
description: Co-founded by economist Kate Raworth, DEAL supports practitioners worldwide in translating doughnut economics from radical idea to transformative action. Works with ~50 cities and regions on local doughnut approaches — redesigning economies to meet social needs within planetary boundaries.
imageAlt: The Doughnut of social and planetary boundaries
about: DEAL translates Kate Raworth's Doughnut Economics from a compelling framework into practical governance reality — supporting cities like Amsterdam and Copenhagen in redesigning their economies around social foundations and planetary boundaries. By working with ~50 cities and regions to develop local Doughnut approaches, they demonstrate that a post-growth economic model is not just theoretically coherent but institutionally implementable. In the planetary governance puzzle, DEAL represents the ASSEMBLY-GARDEN interface: the practical work of embedding ecological limits into the economic governance of real places.

name: Earth Law Center
description: Legal advocacy organisation advancing rights of nature through drafting legislation, supporting court cases, and developing the Universal Declaration of River Rights.
imageAlt: Nature landscape representing rights of nature legal framework
about: Earth Law Center works at the legal frontier of ecological governance — drafting rights of nature legislation, supporting court cases, and developing the Universal Declaration of River Rights. By creating the legal tools and arguments that establish ecosystems as rights-holders rather than resources, they are building the jurisprudential foundations for a legal system that treats the living world as deserving of protection in its own right. They represent the GARDEN-ASSEMBLY interface at its most legally precise: using law as a tool for planetary stewardship.

name: Earth System Governance Project
description: Largest social science network on governance and global environmental change.
imageAlt: Aerial view of urban landscape from above
fundingDisclosure: Academic institutions, Netherlands Organisation for Scientific Research
about: The Earth System Governance Project is the largest social science research network focused on the governance of global environmental change — connecting hundreds of scholars worldwide in a sustained inquiry into how humanity can govern the planet's life-support systems. Their research spans everything from international climate negotiations to local commons governance, providing the academic infrastructure that the planetary governance movement needs to build on solid empirical and theoretical ground. They represent the SPACESHIP-ASSEMBLY interface at its most scholarly: the sustained, global effort to understand governance as a planetary challenge.

name: Ecocentric.earth
description: A whole-system planetary governance consultancy that helps governments and institutions redesign constitutions, policies, and decision-making frameworks to account for the rights of nature, future generations, and the living commons. Operates as a non-hierarchical co-stewardship collective. Applies permaculture, indigenous land stewardship, and digital commons perspectives.
about: Ecocentric.earth works at the intersection of constitutional design and ecological governance — helping governments and institutions redesign decision-making frameworks to account for the rights of nature, future generations, and the living commons. Drawing on permaculture principles, indigenous land stewardship, and digital commons governance, they offer a whole-system approach to planetary governance reform. In the puzzle, they represent the GARDEN-ASSEMBLY intersection at its most structurally ambitious: changing the constitutional frameworks through which societies relate to the living world.

name: Ecosystem Restoration Communities
description: Global movement building community-led ecosystem restoration projects from Brazil to Morocco, connecting local action with the UN Decade on Ecosystem Restoration.
imageAlt: Contour lines in a restoration project in Guatemala
about: Ecosystem Restoration Communities is building a global movement of community-led restoration projects — from reforestation in Brazil to dry-land recovery in Morocco — connecting local action with the UN Decade on Ecosystem Restoration and building the movement infrastructure that can scale it. By centering community ownership and leadership in restoration work, they embed ecological recovery in social recovery. In the planetary governance puzzle, they represent the GARDEN orientation at its most grassroots and most globally networked: the distributed, bottom-up restoration engine that complements top-down policy.

name: Electionville
description: A participatory board game designed by Max Valentin to build democratic literacy — tested with elected officials in Zambia to help communities experience governance as collective sense-making.
about: Electionville sits at the intersection of game design and civic education, using play to make the complexity of democratic systems tangible and navigable. By creating an experience of governance rather than just describing it, it builds the civic intuition that formal education rarely achieves. In the planetary governance context, tools like Electionville represent the MYSTERIES dimension: the insight that culture change and political change are inseparable, and that people need to feel democracy before they can effectively build it.
imageAlt: Globe of Earth highlighting Sweden and sharing culture

name: Elysium Life
description: Immersive theatre experience in Stockholm disguised as a transcendence clinic. Visitors surrender phones, don robes, and undergo a multi-step experiential journey blending salon aesthetics with mythological narrative. Founded by the character Hades.
imageAlt: Elysium Clinic salon
about: Elysium Life creates immersive theatre experiences that use the form of a 'transcendence clinic' to invite participants into altered states of perception — blending salon aesthetics, mythology, and carefully designed ritual to create something between art installation and transformative ceremony. In the planetary governance context, they represent the MYSTERIES orientation at its most experiential: the exploration of inner states and altered consciousness as dimensions of the cultural transformation that systemic change requires. They point to the possibility that how we experience the world is as politically important as what we think about it.

name: Fabel
description: A narrative design studio specialising in storytelling, game design, and experiential futures — creating experiences that help communities imagine and rehearse different ways of living together.
about: Fabel works at the boundary of story and strategy, using narrative as a tool for exploring futures that don't yet exist but could. In a world saturated with dystopian imagination, studios like Fabel are part of the cultural infrastructure needed to make regenerative futures feel real and desirable. They represent the MYSTERIES orientation at its most practical: the conviction that changing the stories we tell is foundational to changing the systems we build.
imageAlt: Fabel Entertainment creative workspace and team

name: Fjallbete
description: Pioneering Swedish farm and learning site serving as official Savory Hub in Sweden. Over two decades of holistic management practices for soil regeneration. Driving force behind Nordic Network for Regenerative Agriculture.
about: Fjallbete is one of Sweden's most important living examples of holistic land management — a working farm that has practiced regenerative grazing and soil restoration for over two decades, serving as a Savory Hub and driving force behind the Nordic Network for Regenerative Agriculture. By combining practical farming with education and network-building, Fjallbete demonstrates at farm scale what the planetary transition to regenerative agriculture looks like in practice. They represent the GARDEN orientation at its most rooted: not a framework or a policy, but a living place where human and ecological health are being rebuilt together.

name: Foodprint Nordic
description: Environmental non-profit dedicated to facilitating food system transition by directing funds and resources toward regenerative farming projects in the Nordic region. Launched Top 50 Farmers initiative.
imageAlt: Food system photography from Foodprint Nordic
about: Foodprint Nordic works to accelerate the transition to regenerative food systems in the Nordic region — directing resources toward regenerative farming projects and building public awareness through initiatives like the Top 50 Farmers programme. By connecting consumers, institutions, and farmers around shared values of ecological health and food sovereignty, they build the market and cultural infrastructure that regenerative agriculture needs to scale. In the planetary governance context, they represent the GARDEN-ASSEMBLY interface at the regional food system level: the political economy of what gets grown, how, and who decides.

name: Future Earth
description: A global research network connecting thousands of scientists and institutions to co-produce knowledge for Earth stewardship and transformative sustainability transitions.
about: Future Earth addresses a structural problem in global governance: the people best positioned to understand planetary systems — scientists — are often poorly connected to those making decisions. By building transdisciplinary research networks that span continents and disciplines, Future Earth creates the knowledge commons that planetary governance requires. They are the SPACESHIP at its most collaborative: not just modelling the Earth, but building the scientific community capable of stewarding it.
imageAlt: Future Earth global research
fundingDisclosure: ICSU, ISSC, UN Environment, national funders

name: Future Generations Commissioner for Wales
description: Statutory body established by the Well-being of Future Generations Act, the world's first independent commissioner mandated to advocate for the interests of future generations in government decisions.
imageAlt: Artwork for Future Generations report
about: Wales's Future Generations Commissioner is one of the most significant governance innovations of recent decades: a statutory officer with legal authority to hold Welsh public bodies accountable for the long-term wellbeing of future generations. Established by the Well-being of Future Generations Act (2015), this unique institution demonstrates that the structural bias toward short-termism in democratic governance can be corrected through institutional design. In the planetary governance puzzle, Wales's Commissioner represents the ASSEMBLY orientation applied to intergenerational justice: proof that future generations can be given a real voice in present decision-making.

name: g0v (gov-zero)
description: A Taiwanese civic tech community that forks government — reimagining official infrastructure with open, participatory alternatives. Founded in 2012, g0v projects include vTaiwan (digital deliberation platform), the open-source budget transparency tool, and dozens of civic data initiatives. A model for citizen-led digital democracy.
about: g0v (gov-zero) is one of the world's most creative civic technology communities — a Taiwanese collective that 'forks' government by building open, participatory alternatives to official digital infrastructure. Their projects, including the vTaiwan digital deliberation platform, have directly shaped national policy and inspired digital democracy movements globally. In the planetary governance context, g0v represents the SPACESHIP-ASSEMBLY intersection at its most inventive: the conviction that the digital infrastructure of governance should be publicly owned, openly auditable, and collaboratively built by the citizens who use it.

name: Gaia AI
description: AI-powered forest management platform providing satellite-derived insights on forest health, carbon stocks, and biodiversity metrics to support data-driven conservation decisions.
imageAlt: Gaia AI satellite and remote sensing technology visualization
about: Gaia AI provides the data intelligence layer for forest governance — using satellite imagery and AI to give land managers, governments, and investors accurate, real-time information about forest health, carbon stocks, and biodiversity. As carbon and biodiversity markets mature, the credibility of the ecological data they're built on becomes increasingly critical. Gaia AI represents the SPACESHIP-GARDEN interface: the remote sensing and machine learning infrastructure that makes forest stewardship legible to the governance systems — financial, regulatory, and democratic — that need to incentivise it.

name: Gaia Foundation
description: UK-based foundation advancing earth jurisprudence, seed sovereignty, and community ecological governance across Africa and South America. Pioneers of the 'earth jurisprudence' legal-philosophical framework.
imageAlt: Soil Sistars session at Love Land community gathering
about: The Gaia Foundation works at the intersection of earth jurisprudence, seed sovereignty, and community ecological governance — advancing a philosophy of law and governance that recognises the Earth as a living community of which humans are part, not masters. Their work across Africa and South America supports communities in asserting their rights to seeds, land, and self-determination against industrial agriculture and extractive industries. They represent the GARDEN-MYSTERIES-ASSEMBLY intersection: the integration of indigenous cosmology, ecological science, and legal advocacy into a coherent vision of how humanity might govern its relationship with the living world.

name: Global Alliance for the Rights of Nature
description: International network advancing rights of nature through legal frameworks, hosting International Rights of Nature Tribunals, and supporting community-led campaigns for ecosystem legal personhood.
imageAlt: Rights of Nature visual identity
about: GARN (the Global Alliance for the Rights of Nature, distinct from GARN/rightsofnaturetribunal.org) is part of the global network building the legal infrastructure for ecosystems to be recognised as rights-holders. By hosting International Rights of Nature Tribunals and supporting community-led campaigns for ecosystem legal personhood, they are contributing to one of the most fundamental legal transformations in history: the shift from treating nature as property to treating it as a subject with inherent rights. They represent the GARDEN-ASSEMBLY interface at its most legally transformative.

name: Global Assembly
description: The world's first global citizens' assembly, bringing together randomly selected people from across the world to deliberate on the climate and ecological crisis. Presented the People's Declaration at COP26 in 2021.
imageAlt: Elderly woman representing intergenerational global voices
about: The Global Assembly was the first experiment in genuine planetary democratic representation — bringing together randomly selected people from every country in the world to deliberate on the climate and ecological crisis and produce a People's Declaration presented at COP26. While one event cannot replace the full architecture of planetary democratic governance, the Global Assembly proved that such an assembly is practically achievable and produces outputs of genuine political quality. In the governance puzzle, it represents both a proof of concept and a template: the ASSEMBLY orientation imagined at the planetary scale and actually attempted.

name: Global Challenges Foundation
description: Swedish foundation offering major prizes for global governance reform proposals, funding research on existential risks and planetary commons, and building networks for institutional innovation.
about: The Global Challenges Foundation takes a long view on civilisational risk — asking which governance failures could lead to catastrophic outcomes and what institutional innovations might prevent them. Their annual New Shape Prize and research programme explore everything from UN reform to existential risk governance, treating the inadequacy of current global institutions as one of the central challenges of our time. In the planetary governance ecosystem, they represent the ASSEMBLY orientation at its most systemic: the conviction that how we collectively decide is as important as what we decide.
imageAlt: Global Threats to Planetary Resilience report cover

name: Global Governance Innovation Network
description: International research network hosted by the Stimson Center, producing annual Global Governance Innovation Reports and connecting researchers working on multilateral system reform.
imageAlt: GGIN event bringing together governance researchers
about: The Global Governance Innovation Network provides the intellectual infrastructure for reforming multilateral institutions — connecting researchers and practitioners working on the redesign of the UN system, international financial architecture, and global regulatory frameworks. Through its annual reports and convenings, GGIN builds the shared analytical vocabulary and political relationships that multilateral reform requires. In the planetary governance context, they represent the ASSEMBLY orientation at its most institutionally sophisticated: the sustained, expert effort to make global governance adequate to global challenges.

name: Global Rewilding Alliance
description: International alliance coordinating rewilding efforts across continents, advocating for rewilding as climate policy and bridging biodiversity and climate agendas.
imageAlt: Scimitar-horned oryx reintroduced to the Sahel
about: The Global Rewilding Alliance is the international architecture of the rewilding movement — coordinating rewilding efforts across continents and advocating for rewilding as both a climate policy and a biodiversity strategy. By bridging the biodiversity and climate agendas, they argue that ecological restoration is not a niche conservation concern but a core element of the planetary governance response. In the puzzle, they represent the GARDEN orientation at its most coordinated: the global infrastructure for connecting, scaling, and legitimising the rewilding revolution.

name: Global Sustainability Jam
description: A globally decentralised 48-hour design sprint run simultaneously across dozens of cities, challenging teams to tackle sustainability challenges through rapid collaborative prototyping.
about: The Global Sustainability Jam embodies the principle that distributed, bottom-up action can be globally coordinated without central control. By running the same creative challenge simultaneously in dozens of cities, it builds a shared experience of collaborative problem-solving while generating diverse local solutions. In the planetary governance context, the Jam represents an experiment in what commons-based, leaderless global coordination can actually look like in practice — and a training ground for the collaborative muscle that planetary challenges require.
imageAlt: Participants collaborating at a Global Sustainability Jam event

name: Global Tapestry of Alternatives
description: A network of networks initiated in 2019, emerging from India's Vikalp Sangam (Alternatives Confluence) to create solidarity and strategic alliances among radical transformation movements worldwide. Coordinated by Ashish Kothari and colleagues, it maps and connects alternatives to dominant development paradigms — from agroecology and commons governance to autonomous indigenous communities — across regions and themes.
about: The Global Tapestry of Alternatives, coordinated by Ashish Kothari, is a radical mapping and solidarity project — connecting the thousands of communities worldwide that are already practicing alternatives to dominant development paradigms, from autonomous indigenous governance to commons-based agroecology to feminist cooperative economies. Rather than proposing a single alternative, it celebrates and connects the pluriverse of actually-existing alternatives. In the planetary governance context, it represents all four orientations in integration: the recognition that the planetary transition is not a single project but a vast, diverse, self-organising movement of people already building different worlds.

name: Governance Futures
description: Organisation creating experiential and world-building workshops that engage communities in radical imagination about governance futures, using art, illustration, and comic books to envision equitable decision-making.
imageAlt: Governance Futures hero imagery and visual identity
about: Governance Futures uses art, illustration, and experiential workshops to invite communities into radical imagination about democratic futures. By making governance tangible through comic books, visual storytelling, and collaborative world-building, they address a specific gap in the planetary governance movement: the difficulty of imagining genuinely different political arrangements when the existing ones feel so naturalised. They represent the MYSTERIES-ASSEMBLY intersection: cultural and artistic tools in service of democratic imagination, making it possible to want political change before you can plan it.

name: Grounded Solutions Network
description: National network supporting community land trusts and shared equity housing, advancing community ownership models that keep land in common stewardship.
imageAlt: Community land trust housing in urban neighborhood
about: Grounded Solutions Network works on one of the most fundamental questions of planetary governance at the local level: who owns the land, and under what terms? By supporting community land trusts and shared equity housing models, they build the commons-based ownership infrastructure that keeps land in community stewardship rather than financial speculation. In the planetary governance context, they represent the GARDEN-ASSEMBLY interface applied to urban and rural land: demonstrating that community ownership is a practical and scalable alternative to both state and market control of land.

name: Hypha DAO
description: Human-centric decentralised organisation tools for regenerative projects.
imageAlt: Hypha DAO platform interface for decentralized organization building
about: Hypha DAO builds the human-centric organisational tools for the regenerative economy — providing decentralised governance infrastructure that allows communities, cooperatives, and mission-driven organisations to coordinate complex work without centralised management. By designing for human values rather than speculation, Hypha represents the ReFi ecosystem's attempt to make Web3 governance actually usable by the organisations that need it most. In the planetary governance puzzle, they occupy the SPACESHIP-ASSEMBLY interface: the practical governance technology that makes decentralised coordination operationally real.

name: IIED Biocultural Heritage
description: International Institute for Environment and Development research programme on biocultural heritage, bridging indigenous knowledge systems, biodiversity conservation, and community rights.
about: IIED's Biocultural Heritage programme works at the intersection of biodiversity, indigenous knowledge, and community rights — recognising that the cultural practices and knowledge systems of indigenous and local communities are inseparable from the biological diversity they have co-created and stewarded. By researching and advocating for biocultural approaches in international conservation and development policy, they challenge the separation of nature and culture that has characterised mainstream conservation. They represent the GARDEN-MYSTERIES intersection: the recognition that ecological governance must honour the cultural knowledge that has sustained living landscapes.

name: Institute for Futures Studies
description: Interdisciplinary research organization investigating long-term societal threats and living conditions of future generations, focusing on climate ethics, sustainable development, and social dilemmas.
imageAlt: Researcher studying digital democracy at IFFS
about: The Institute for Futures Studies applies rigorous interdisciplinary research to the long-term challenges facing humanity — from climate ethics and intergenerational justice to sustainable development and social dilemmas. Based in Stockholm and connected to international networks, they provide the academic infrastructure for thinking seriously about governance on the timescales that ecological challenges require. In the planetary governance context, they represent the SPACESHIP-ASSEMBLY interface applied to deep time: the intellectual work of understanding what our obligations to the future are, and how present institutions can be redesigned to meet them.

name: Institute for the Future
description: 50+ year old futures research institute running experiential futures training, foresight research, and large-scale public forecasting exercises combining gameplay with futures thinking.
imageAlt: IFTF foresight event with participants engaged in futures thinking
about: The Institute for the Future has spent over 50 years developing the practice of futures thinking — combining rigorous foresight research with innovative experiential futures tools that help organisations and communities navigate complex, rapidly changing landscapes. Their large-scale forecasting games and futures simulations have engaged millions of people in thinking seriously about long-term challenges. In the planetary governance context, they represent the MYSTERIES-SPACESHIP interface: using game design and experiential futures to cultivate the anticipatory imagination that governance for a complex, uncertain planet requires.

name: Kate Raworth / Doughnut Economics
description: The creator of Doughnut Economics — a framework for reimagining economic success as meeting human needs within planetary boundaries, now adopted by cities and governments worldwide.
imageAlt: Doughnut Economics visual framework
about: Kate Raworth's Doughnut model offers something rare in economics: a framework that is simultaneously rigorous, visual, and politically actionable. By defining a 'safe and just space for humanity' bounded by social foundations below and ecological ceilings above, it gives policymakers a north star that integrates social justice and ecological limits into a single governance metric. Her work has moved from academic provocation to practical policy in Amsterdam, Copenhagen, and beyond — demonstrating that better frameworks genuinely change decisions.

name: Kindship
description: AI platform building autonomous agents for mission-driven individuals and teams. Agents hold direction over time, act independently, remember context, and produce real-world outputs — designed for changemakers, sensemakers, and collaborative projects.
imageAlt: Kindship AI platform
about: Kindship is building AI agents specifically for the people driving systemic change — changemakers, sensemakers, and collaborative projects working on planetary challenges. By creating autonomous agents that can hold direction over time, act independently, and remember context, Kindship addresses a real bottleneck for social change organisations: the gap between the complexity of the challenges they face and the operational capacity they have to address them. In the planetary governance context, they represent the SPACESHIP orientation applied to the infrastructure of change-making itself: AI as a tool for amplifying the impact of those working toward planetary stewardship.

name: Kiss the Ground
description: Soil health advocacy NGO producing documentary films, running farmer training programmes, and building public awareness of regenerative agriculture as a climate solution.
about: Kiss the Ground has made soil health visible to millions through documentary filmmaking, farmer training, and popular advocacy — building the public understanding of regenerative agriculture as a climate solution that policy change requires. By making the science of soil carbon sequestration emotionally compelling and accessible, they address a critical bottleneck in the ecological transition: the gap between what the science says and what the public and policymakers prioritise. In the planetary governance puzzle, they represent the GARDEN-MYSTERIES interface: using storytelling and cultural change to build the political constituency for soil stewardship.

name: KNOCA
description: EU-funded Knowledge Network on Climate Assemblies, connecting organisers, researchers, and policymakers working on climate citizens' assemblies across Europe.
imageAlt: European deliberative democracy conference participants
about: KNOCA is the knowledge infrastructure for the European citizens' assembly movement — connecting organisers, researchers, and policymakers working on climate citizens' assemblies across EU member states. By building a shared knowledge base on what works, what doesn't, and what different national contexts require, KNOCA accelerates learning across the rapidly growing field. In the planetary governance context, they represent the ASSEMBLY orientation applied to its own development: the meta-infrastructure that helps deliberative democracy practitioners learn from each other and improve their practice.

name: Kolektivo
description: A platform building local network economies using nature-based currencies and community governance tools to reward ecological stewardship and enable community-controlled UBI.
about: Kolektivo experiments with a fundamental question: what if economic reward and ecological value were directly aligned? By creating local currencies backed by verified ecosystem services and governed by community assemblies, it prototypes an economy where taking care of the land is literally profitable. In the planetary governance puzzle, Kolektivo represents the SPACESHIP-GARDEN interface: using Web3 infrastructure to make regenerative economies computationally legible and governable at community scale.
imageAlt: Kolektivo community in Curacao

name: Learning Planet Institute
description: Paris-based institute developing educational approaches for planetary citizenship, running futures learning labs on anticipatory governance and connecting education with sustainability transformation.
imageAlt: Youth engagement at Learning Planet
about: The Learning Planet Institute is building the education system for planetary citizenship — developing learning approaches that cultivate the knowledge, values, and capacities needed to navigate and co-create civilisational transformation. Their futures learning labs and anticipatory governance programmes work with young people and educators worldwide. In the planetary governance context, they represent the MYSTERIES-SPACESHIP interface applied to education: the conviction that the transition requires not just better governance systems but better-educated citizens who can participate meaningfully in them.

name: Linköping University Climate Change Megagame
description: Swedish academic megagame project at Linköping University simulating regional climate transformation 2020-2100, using SMHI forecast data to create participatory governance simulations.
imageAlt: Climate change megagame session at Linköping University
about: Linköping University's Climate Change Megagame turns rigorous climate science into lived political experience — using real SMHI forecast data to simulate regional governance scenarios from 2020 to 2100, placing participants in the roles of decision-makers navigating the actual complexity of climate transition. By making the future tangible and political in a rigorous academic context, it demonstrates the potential of serious games as tools for both research and public engagement. They represent the MYSTERIES-SPACESHIP interface at its most academically grounded: using play to make science governable.

name: Liquid Democracy e.V.
description: Berlin-based nonprofit developing open-source digital participation tools including Adhocracy, enabling liquid democracy and deliberative processes for governments and civil society.
imageAlt: Liquid Democracy organization banner and visual identity
about: Liquid Democracy e.V. develops and deploys open-source digital participation tools — including the Adhocracy platform — that enable liquid democracy and structured deliberation for governments and civil society. Liquid democracy, which allows citizens to either vote directly or delegate their vote to trusted proxies on specific issues, offers a compelling model for scaling democratic participation without sacrificing depth. In the planetary governance context, Liquid Democracy represents the SPACESHIP-ASSEMBLY interface: technical innovation in service of democratic scalability.

name: Long Now Foundation
description: An institution cultivating long-term thinking and civilisational responsibility over a 10,000-year horizon — through the Clock of the Long Now, seminars, and the Long Bets platform.
about: The Long Now Foundation exists to counteract one of governance's deepest pathologies: short-termism. By building a literal 10,000-year clock, maintaining long-term archives, and hosting conversations about civilisational trajectory, it nurtures a culture where decisions are evaluated on generational rather than quarterly timescales. For planetary governance, the Long Now represents the MYSTERIES dimension of temporal imagination: helping communities and institutions develop the long-sightedness that stewardship of a living planet requires.
imageAlt: Long Now Foundation hero image

name: Martin Ericsson
description: A pioneering Nordic game designer and LARP creator whose work explores how designed experiences can shift social imagination, build empathy, and cultivate new forms of collective intelligence.
about: Martin Ericsson's work — from Vampire: The Masquerade to boundary-pushing Nordic LARPs — has demonstrated for decades that immersive play is a powerful technology for cultural change. By creating experiences that put participants inside different social arrangements, relationships, and value systems, he has shown how games can do what arguments rarely can: shift how people feel about the world. In the planetary governance context, his work exemplifies the MYSTERIES orientation: the use of play and story as tools for social transformation, building the imaginative capacity that political transformation requires.
imageAlt: Martin Ericsson speaking at a conference

name: Megagame Assembly
description: International community hub for megagame design and play, connecting designers and organisers of large-scale multi-player simulation games that blend strategy, diplomacy, and governance.
imageAlt: Players engaged in a large-scale megagame event
about: Megagame Assembly is the international hub for megagame design and play — connecting the designers and organisers of large-scale multi-player simulation games that put dozens or hundreds of participants inside complex systems of diplomacy, governance, and resource management. Megagames are unique tools for building systemic intuition: by experiencing the dynamics of collective decision-making from the inside, players develop governance capacities that abstract learning cannot produce. They represent the MYSTERIES-ASSEMBLY intersection: play as a technology for building political imagination and collective intelligence.

name: Megagame Coalition
description: US-based network of megagame organisers collaborating on large-scale simulation game design, production, and community building across North America.
imageAlt: Megagame event with players gathered around tables
about: The Megagame Coalition is the North American community of megagame practice — connecting designers and organisers across the continent who are using large-scale simulation gaming to explore governance, strategy, and collective decision-making. As megagames gain recognition as serious tools for education, training, and public engagement, the Coalition provides the community infrastructure that allows the field to develop and professionalise. They represent the MYSTERIES-ASSEMBLY orientation: the use of immersive play to build the governance capacities and political imagination that planetary challenges require.

name: Metagov
description: Research collective studying and building interoperable governance tools for online communities, exploring governance games and tool experimentation across digital and physical spaces.
about: Metagov is the research infrastructure for the emerging field of digital self-governance — studying how online communities, DAOs, platforms, and digital institutions make collective decisions, resolve conflicts, and build legitimacy. As more of human coordination moves into digital spaces, questions of digital governance become questions of planetary governance. Metagov represents the SPACESHIP-ASSEMBLY interface at its most rigorous: the application of empirical research and democratic theory to the governance of the networks that increasingly shape collective life.
imageAlt: Metagov dithered globe graphic

name: Movement Generation Justice & Ecology Project
description: A US nonprofit collective rooted in the wisdom of frontline communities, working toward the liberation and restoration of land, labour, and culture through ecological justice. Developed the Resilience-Based Organizing model and the Just Transition framework (co-created with Climate Justice Alliance). Holds the principle that "if we're not prepared to govern, we're not prepared to win."
about: Movement Generation is rooted in the wisdom of frontline communities — the people most directly harmed by extractive economic systems and ecological breakdown — and insists that any just transition must be led by them. Their Resilience-Based Organizing model and the Just Transition framework (co-created with the Climate Justice Alliance) have become foundational documents in the global climate justice movement. Their guiding principle — 'if we're not prepared to govern, we're not prepared to win' — captures the ASSEMBLY orientation at its most politically urgent: the insistence that movement-building and governance-building are inseparable.

name: Naturskyddsföreningen
description: Sweden's largest environmental membership organisation, advocating for biodiversity protection, ecological transition, and nature's rights across Swedish civil society and policy.
imageAlt: Sweeping Swedish landscape with forests and mountains
about: With over 200,000 members, Naturskyddsföreningen (the Swedish Society for Nature Conservation) is one of the oldest and most influential environmental organisations in the Nordic region. It combines grassroots campaigning with policy expertise, making it a key actor in translating ecological science into political change at the national level. For the planetary governance movement, it represents the GARDEN orientation at its most embedded: a mass membership organisation with the legitimacy and longevity to hold governments accountable on ecological commitments.

name: New Economy Network Australia
description: A national cooperative network of organisations and individuals working to build a fair, sustainable, and democratic economy in Australia. Connects community energy, cooperative housing, ethical finance, regenerative food systems, and local circular economies through sectoral hubs. Has operated for a decade as a model of networked economic transformation.
about: The New Economy Network Australia is building the connective tissue for economic transformation in Australia — linking community energy cooperatives, ethical finance institutions, regenerative food systems, cooperative housing, and local circular economies into a national network with shared values and mutual support. By operating as a cooperative network rather than a traditional NGO, it embodies the alternative economics it advocates for. In the planetary governance context, they represent the ASSEMBLY-GARDEN interface applied to economic transition: demonstrating that a different economy is not only theoretically possible but practically under construction.

name: Nordic Blockchain Alliance
description: Cross-border initiative funded by Nordic Innovation exploring joint Nordic blockchain ecosystem, targeting lifestyle and design industries for circular business model transition and European Product Passport compliance.
about: The Nordic Blockchain Alliance explores how blockchain technology can support the circular economy and sustainable supply chains in Nordic lifestyle and design industries — working on European Product Passport compliance and circular business model transition. While blockchain's most prominent applications have been financial speculation, the Nordic Blockchain Alliance focuses on governance applications: using distributed ledger technology to create transparency and accountability in supply chains. They represent the SPACESHIP orientation applied to industrial governance: digital infrastructure for circular economy accountability.

name: Nordic Deliberation
description: Coalition of four leading democracy organizations across Scandinavia: We Do Democracy (Denmark), Sitra (Finland), Digidem Lab (Sweden), and SoCentral (Norway). Accelerating the deliberative wave across the Nordic region.
about: Nordic Deliberation is building the regional infrastructure for citizens' assemblies and deliberative democracy across Scandinavia — connecting national organisations in Denmark, Norway, Sweden, and Finland to share methods, research, and political advocacy. The Nordic region has a strong tradition of participatory governance, and this initiative aims to extend and systematise it for the challenges of the ecological transition. In the planetary governance puzzle, they represent the ASSEMBLY orientation as a regional project: demonstrating that deliberative democracy can be institutionalised, not just practiced occasionally.

name: Not Only Larp
description: Italian design studio creating immersive LARP experiences explicitly for social change, running large-scale educational and political LARPs across Europe.
imageAlt: Large-scale political LARP event
about: Not Only Larp is an Italian design studio that has made social change through immersive gaming its explicit purpose — running large-scale educational and political LARPs across Europe that use the visceral, embodied logic of roleplay to engage participants with governance challenges, social justice themes, and political scenarios. By combining theatrical craft with genuine political intent, they demonstrate that LARP is not just entertainment but a serious tool for civic education and social transformation. They represent the MYSTERIES orientation at its most explicitly political: play as a vehicle for consciousness change.

name: Open Data Cube
description: Open-source geospatial data management and analysis platform enabling governments and researchers to process and analyse satellite earth observation data at national scale.
imageAlt: Satellite imagery of Earth surface from Open Data Cube
about: The Open Data Cube is infrastructure for planetary vision — an open-source platform that enables governments and researchers to process and analyse satellite earth observation data at national and regional scale, turning the torrent of remote sensing data into actionable environmental intelligence. By making satellite data analysis accessible to the countries that need it most, it democratises the SPACESHIP capacity for planetary monitoring. In the governance context, the ODC represents the technical foundation for evidence-based ecological governance: the ability to see your land from space and act on what you learn.

name: OpenEarth Foundation
description: A nonprofit building open digital infrastructure for climate action — including spatial data systems, biodiversity monitoring tools, and open standards for climate disclosure.
imageAlt: Earth visualization by OpenEarth
about: OpenEarth Foundation works on the often-invisible layer that makes planetary governance computationally possible: the open data infrastructure for tracking, verifying, and disclosing ecological information. From spatial mesh tools to active inference frameworks for biodiversity monitoring, their work creates the technical commons that cities, governments, and companies need to make credible environmental commitments. They represent the SPACESHIP at its most infrastructural — not building applications, but building the open protocols that all applications can build on.

name: P2P Foundation
description: A global research and advocacy network founded by Michel Bauwens in 2005, dedicated to commons-oriented peer-to-peer dynamics across society, economy, and governance. Maintains an 8,000-page wiki on peer production, commons governance, and open-source alternatives. Provides theoretical grounding for platform cooperativism, digital commons, and post-capitalist transition.
about: The P2P Foundation, founded by Michel Bauwens, has spent two decades building the theoretical and practical commons of peer-to-peer governance — maintaining a vast wiki of knowledge on commons governance, platform cooperativism, open-source production, and post-capitalist alternatives. Their concept of 'commons-based peer production' has influenced digital rights movements, cooperative economy practitioners, and governance innovators worldwide. In the planetary governance puzzle, the P2P Foundation represents the ASSEMBLY-SPACESHIP interface at its most intellectually generative: the sustained effort to understand and spread the governance principles that allow distributed communities to produce and steward shared resources.

name: Participatory Budgeting Project
description: Leading US organisation advancing participatory budgeting as a democratic practice, having supported over 400 PB processes and helped allocate hundreds of millions in public funds through direct citizen decision-making.
about: The Participatory Budgeting Project has taken one of democracy's most powerful innovations — giving citizens direct control over public spending decisions — and built the organisational capacity to implement it across hundreds of cities. By making budgeting participatory, it transforms a technical administrative process into an exercise of democratic citizenship. In the planetary governance context, PBP represents the ASSEMBLY orientation at its most concrete and transferable: a democratic practice that can be implemented in almost any city and that demonstrably shifts both outcomes and civic culture.
imageAlt: Community members engaging in participatory budgeting process

name: PAXsims
description: Community hub and publication devoted to peace, conflict, humanitarian, and development simulations and serious games for education, training, and policy analysis.
imageAlt: Connections North simulation and gaming event
about: PAXsims is the community hub and publication of record for peace, conflict, and development simulations — connecting the designers and users of serious games used in humanitarian training, policy analysis, and peacebuilding education worldwide. By documenting, critiquing, and connecting simulation practice across these fields, PAXsims builds the collective knowledge base of a field that uses play for some of the most consequential purposes imaginable. In the planetary governance context, they represent the MYSTERIES-ASSEMBLY intersection: the rigorous application of game design to the challenges of peace, governance, and collective decision-making under conditions of conflict.

name: People Powered
description: Global hub for participatory democracy connecting practitioners and governments, hosting the Public Participation and Deliberative Democracy Festival, and providing tools and guides for democratic innovation.
imageAlt: People Powered organization promotional image
about: People Powered is the global hub for participatory democracy practice — connecting practitioners, governments, and researchers working on citizens' assemblies, participatory budgeting, and other forms of democratic innovation worldwide. Through their annual Public Participation and Deliberative Democracy Festival and practical toolkits, they build the global community of practice that allows democratic innovation to spread and improve. In the planetary governance ecosystem, People Powered represents the ASSEMBLY orientation at its most globally networked: the infrastructure for a worldwide movement toward deeper, more genuine democracy.

name: Planetary Guardians / SpiralWeb
description: A governance innovation initiative developing frameworks for land stewards and communities to collectively manage natural commons, grounded in Ostrom's principles for self-organised resource governance.
imageAlt: Planetary Guardians visual identity showing Earth from space
about: Planetary Guardians / SpiralWeb works on the governance architecture for those who live with and care for natural systems — developing frameworks that allow land stewards to coordinate collective management of shared ecosystems without either state control or market extraction. Drawing on Ostrom's work on the commons and pattern languages for ecological governance, their three-stream model integrates monitoring, deliberation, and adaptive management. They represent the GARDEN-ASSEMBLY bridge: the conviction that healthy land requires healthy governance, and that communities are often the most effective stewards when given the right tools and legitimate authority.

name: Planetary Health Alliance
description: Global consortium of 400+ universities, NGOs, and government entities advancing planetary health — the interdisciplinary framework linking human health outcomes to planetary ecosystem changes.
imageAlt: Promise of Planetary Health video still
about: The Planetary Health Alliance is building the scientific and institutional infrastructure for planetary health — the interdisciplinary framework that treats human health as inseparable from the health of the ecosystems, climate systems, and ecological processes that sustain it. With 400+ universities, NGOs, and government partners, they represent the academic mainstream of the GARDEN-SPACESHIP interface: the epidemiological, ecological, and systems science that demonstrates, with medical rigour, why governing the planet's living systems is a matter of public health.

name: Plurality Institute
description: Research institute founded by Glen Weyl advancing plural technology — collaborative tools for cooperation across difference, including quadratic voting/funding, plural money, and decentralised identity systems.
imageAlt: Cover of the Plurality book by Glen Weyl and collaborators
about: The Plurality Institute advances plural technology — collaborative tools for cooperation across difference — as an alternative to both centralised AI and fragmented individualism. Building on Glen Weyl's work on quadratic mechanisms, decentralised identity, and plural governance, the Institute develops the theoretical and technical foundations for digital systems that strengthen diverse, democratic communities rather than homogenising or polarising them. In the planetary governance context, they represent the SPACESHIP-ASSEMBLY interface at its most philosophically ambitious: the conviction that the architecture of digital systems encodes political values, and that building democratic technology requires rethinking those values from the ground up.

name: Polis
description: An open-source platform for large-scale structured conversation that surfaces genuine consensus — used by Taiwan's government and cities worldwide as a participatory policymaking tool.
about: Polis solves a fundamental problem in digital democracy: how to enable thousands of people to have a meaningful conversation without it collapsing into polarisation or noise. By clustering opinions and surfacing statements that find agreement across tribal lines, it reveals the latent consensus that often exists in divided communities. Used in Taiwan's vTaiwan process and adopted by cities and organisations worldwide, Polis represents the SPACESHIP-ASSEMBLY interface: civic technology that makes large-scale collective intelligence computationally tractable.

name: Pro-Human AI Declaration
description: A global coalition articulating that artificial intelligence must serve human values and remain accountable to democratic governance — not autonomous systems or market imperatives alone.
about: As AI systems become more powerful and pervasive, the question of whose values they serve and who governs them becomes a planetary governance question. The Pro-Human AI Declaration articulates a civil society position in this debate: that AI must remain accountable to the humans it affects, and that democratic oversight is non-negotiable. In the planetary governance context, it represents the ASSEMBLY dimension of AI governance — the insistence that technical decisions with civilisational consequences require democratic legitimacy, and that this legitimacy must be actively claimed and defended.
imageAlt: Pro-Human AI Declaration branding and campaign imagery

name: Prototopia
description: A Nordic platform for prototyping new cultural and governance forms — running experiments, gatherings, and projects that explore what regenerative, participatory futures can look and feel like.
about: Prototopia operates on the conviction that transformation requires practice: not just new ideas, but new experiences, rituals, and ways of being together. By creating spaces where alternative forms of governance and culture can be tried, refined, and shared, Prototopia builds the experiential infrastructure for civilisational change. It represents the MYSTERIES-ASSEMBLY intersection: the belief that you need to inhabit new ways of deciding together before you can institutionalise them — and that this inhabiting is itself a political act.
imageAlt: Prototopia futures festival

name: Purpose Foundation
description: A foundation advancing steward-ownership as an alternative to extractive capitalism — designing corporate structures where purpose is legally protected and profit serves mission rather than investors.
about: Purpose Foundation argues that the most durable form of governance reform may be changing the legal architecture of organisations themselves. Steward-owned companies — where control stays with active stewards and profit is reinvested rather than extracted — have shown remarkable resilience and purpose-alignment in practice. In the planetary governance movement, Purpose represents the ASSEMBLY-SPACESHIP interface: using institutional design and legal innovation to create economic actors that can be trusted participants in the ecological transition.
imageAlt: Steward ownership illustration

name: Radical Ecological Democracy
